request.question: Imiliya is a town located in what pilgrimage site?

title: Kushinagar
Pushkar
Dobbaspet
Gajpanth
Kundalpur
Lumbini
Imiliya
Chakia, Bihar
Giruwari
Grishneshwar
sentences: Kushinagar, Kusinagar or Kusinara is a pilgrimage town and a Notified Area Council in the Kushinagar district of the Indian state of Uttar Pradesh located around NH-28, and is 52 km east of Gorakhpur city.
It is an important Buddhist pilgrimage site, where Buddhists believe Gautama Buddha attained Parinirvana after his death.
Outside India, it is an International Buddhist Pilgrimage Centre.
The followers of Buddhism, especially from Asian countries, wish to visit this place at least once in their lifetime.
Pushkar (Hindi: पुष्कर ) is a town in the Ajmer district in the Indian state of Rajasthan.
It is situated 14 km northwest of Ajmer at an average elevation of 510 m and is one of the five sacred dhams (pilgrimage site) for devout Hindus.

According to Hindu theology, the pond at the Katas Raj Temples near Choa Saidan Shah in Chakwal District of Pakistan has a theological association with Shiva; it was formed by the tears of Lord Shiva which he is believed to have shed after the death of his wife, Sati.

The story goes that when Sati died, Shiva cried so much and for so long, that his tears created two holy ponds – one at Pushkara in Ajmer in India and the other at Ketaksha, which literally means raining eyes, in Sanskrit.
It is from this name that the word Katas is derived.
It is often called "Tirth Raj" – the king of pilgrimage sites – and has in recent years become a popular destination for foreign tourists.
Dobbspet is a tiny town located in Nelamangala Taluk, Bangalore Rural District, Karnataka, India.

formerly called as Somapura re named as Dobbspet in memory of Major General Richard Stewart Dobbs first collector of Tumkur District, who is an eminent statesman who devoted his major part of life for the service of the People by the establishment of order and systematic administration in Tumkur district.
Dobbspet is located 50 km away from Bangalore along National Highway 4.
Nearest city is Tumkur.
Hindu pilgrimage site of Shivagange lies near the town.
Gajpanth (also spelled "Gajpantha") is a Jain pilgrimage site ("Sidh-ak-shetra") located near the Jain temple at Nashik.
It is located in Masrul village, in the Nashik district of the Indian state of Maharashtra.
It is 16 km from the Nashik Road Railway station and 5 km from Nashik City, and is situated on the steep slopes of a 400 ft hill.
The pilgrimage site is accessible via a staircase built in black stone, which leads directly to the temple.
The hill has 450 steps, three caves (known as "' chamar leni' "), and several temples belonging to the Digambara sect of the Jains.
There is also a sculpture depicting samavasarana (divine preaching hall of tirthankara) on the hills of Gajpanth.
Kundalpur is a town located in the Indian state of Madhya Pradesh, 35 km from the city of Damoh.
It is a historical pilgrimage site for Jains in India.
Lumbinī (Nepali and Sanskrit: लुम्बिनी    , "the lovely") is a Buddhist pilgrimage site in the Rupandehi District of Nepal.
It is the place where, according to Buddhist tradition, Queen Mayadevi gave birth to Siddhartha Gautama in 563 BCE.
Gautama, who achieved Enlightenment some time around 528 BCE, became the Buddha and founded Buddhism.
Lumbini is one of many magnets for pilgrimage that sprang up in places pivotal to the life of the Buddha.
Imiliya इमिलिया is a small town located in the Kapilvastu District, Lumbini, Nepal.
Chakia is one of the important and commercial towns and a Nagar Panchayat in Purvi Champaran district in the state of Bihar, India.
Chakia is sub divisional headquarter town and also block headquarter.
During the British rule, Champaran Sugar Mill was founded in 1905.
sugar cane mill is now defunct since 1995.
Chakia is one of the many gateways to nearby villages.
Cloths and food grain traded between near by villages and Chakia.
Chakia now is a rapidly growing town.

Chakia has many old and famous places of worship like Shree Rani Sati Mandir, Baudhi Devi Mandir, Balkhana Devi Mandir, Durga Mandir, Maruti Mandir (Bife Bazaar), Hanumaan Mandir (Bara Bazaar), Noori Masjid (Near Railway Station), Jama Masjid and many more.
The town down area of chakia are Keshria Road, Sahebganj Road, Station Road, Motihari Road, Hospital Road and Madhuban Road.
There areas are densely populated with small shops as well as branded or non-branded shop selling various types of goods and services.
The associate Post Office is called Bara Chakia.
This town is served by a broad gauge train and connected by NH-28.
The railway station is called Chakia.
It is also the closest railway station to Kesaria Stupa, a Buddhist pilgrimage site.
Giruwari sometimes known as Girwari Jhyalbas ( गिरुवारी ) is one of the village located in north part of Nawalparasi district of Lumnini Zone in the western development region of Nepal.
Lumbinī (Sanskrit: लुम्बिनी, "the lovely") is a Buddhist pilgrimage site in the Rupandehi district of Nepal.
[1] It is the place where Queen Mayadevi gave birth to Siddhartha Gautama, who is the Buddha.
Gautama founded the Buddhist tradition.
Buddha lived between roughly 563 and 483 BC.
Lumbini is one of four magnets for pilgrimage that sprang up in places pivotal to the life of the Buddha, the others being at Kushinagar, Bodh Gaya and Sarnath.
Grishneshwar temple, sometimes referred to as the Ghrneshwar Jyotirlinga or Dhushmeshwar temple, is one of the 12 Jyotirlinga shrines mentioned in the Shiva Purana.
The word "Ghrneshwar" means "lord of compassion".
The temple is an important pilgrimage site in Shaivism tradition of Hinduism, which considers it as the last or twelfth Jyotirlinga (linga of light).
This pilgrimage site is located in Ellora (also called Verul), less than a kilometer from Ellora Caves – a UNESCO World Heritage site.
It is about 30 km north-west of the city of Aurangabad, and about 300 km east-northeast from Mumbai.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Imiliya is a town located in what pilgrimage site?
hop1_docs: Imiliya | Imiliya इमिलिया is a small town located in the Kapilvastu District, Lumbini, Nepal.
Madhu, Sri Lanka | Madhu is town in Mannar District, Sri Lanka.  The Shrine of Our Lady of Madhu, a major site of Roman Catholic pilgrimage, is located in this town.

Jison-in | Jison-in (慈尊院 ) is a Buddhist temple in the town of Kudoyama that marks the entrance to the pilgrimage route of Koyasan.  It is part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Kushinagar | Kushinagar, Kusinagar or Kusinara is a pilgrimage town and a Notified Area Council in the Kushinagar district of the Indian state of Uttar Pradesh located around NH-28, and is 52 km east of Gorakhpur city.  It is an important Buddhist pilgrimage site, where Buddhists believe Gautama Buddha attained Parinirvana after his death.  Outside India, it is an International Buddhist Pilgrimage Centre.  The followers of Buddhism, especially from Asian countries, wish to visit this place at least once in their lifetime.
Kundalpur | Kundalpur is a town located in the Indian state of Madhya Pradesh, 35 km from the city of Damoh.  It is a historical pilgrimage site for Jains in India.

Yoshino Mountain | Yoshino Mountain (吉野山 , Yoshino-yama ) is a mountain located in the town of Yoshino in Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture, Japan.  In 2004, It was designated as part of a UNESCO World Heritage Site under the name "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range".

Reward, Saskatchewan | 12 km south of Reward on Grid 675 is the site of the Shrine of the Holy Rosary. .  The site has been the location of an annual pilgrimage since 1932. About 3,000 people participated in the first pilgrimage.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found: Imiliya is mentioned in passage [1]: "small town located in the Kapilvastu District, Lumbini, Nepal." (passage [1]).
- Ambiguities: Only one Imiliya entry appears (passage [1]) — no alternative Imiliya pages in the provided passages. However, the passages do not explicitly call "Lumbini" a pilgrimage site; other pilgrimage pages (e.g., "Kushinagar" [4], "Kundalpur" [5], "Jison-in" [3]) exist but are not linked to Imiliya.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact: Bridge entity = "Lumbini" (as shown in passage [1]). Missing fact needed = an explicit statement that "Lumbini" is a pilgrimage site (its canonical pilgrimage designation and/or lede description).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open the "Lumbini" page (lede/infobox and first paragraphs) — check for wording like "pilgrimage", "birthplace of", or "major Buddhist pilgrimage site".
     - Search keywords: "Lumbini lede infobox pilgrimage", "Lumbini Buddhist pilgrimage site".
  2) Open "Lumbini, Nepal" / "Lumbini (UNESCO World Heritage Site)" pages (History/Significance sections) to confirm its pilgrimage status and canonical name.
  3) Optionally open "Kapilvastu District" page lede/places/settlements list to confirm Imiliya's administrative placement if needed.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
response.hop2_query: Lumbini pilgrimage site lede birthplace of Buddha
hop2_docs: Lumbini | Lumbinī (Nepali and Sanskrit: लुम्बिनी    , "the lovely") is a Buddhist pilgrimage site in the Rupandehi District of Nepal.  It is the place where, according to Buddhist tradition, Queen Mayadevi gave birth to Siddhartha Gautama in 563 BCE.  Gautama, who achieved Enlightenment some time around 528 BCE, became the Buddha and founded Buddhism.  Lumbini is one of many magnets for pilgrimage that sprang up in places pivotal to the life of the Buddha.

Lumbini Zone | Lumbini (Nepali:    ) is one of the fourteen zones of Nepal and home to the Lumbini site, the birthplace of Siddhartha Gautama, who later became the historical Buddha and founder of the Buddhist philosophy.  The zone's headquarters is Butwal.

Maya Devi Temple, Lumbini | Maya Devi Temple is an ancient Buddhist temple situated at the UNESCO World Heritage Site of Lumbini, Nepal.  It is the main temple at Lumbini, a site traditionally considered the birthplace of Gautama Buddha.  The temple stands adjacent to a sacred pool (known as Puskarni) and a sacred garden.  The archaeological remains at the site were previously dated to the third-century BCE brick buildings constructed by Ashoka.  A sixth-century BCE timber shrine was discovered in 2013.

Giruwari | Giruwari sometimes known as Girwari Jhyalbas ( गिरुवारी ) is one of the village located in north part of Nawalparasi district of Lumnini Zone in the western development region of Nepal.  Lumbinī (Sanskrit: लुम्बिनी, "the lovely") is a Buddhist pilgrimage site in the Rupandehi district of Nepal. [1] It is the place where Queen Mayadevi gave birth to Siddhartha Gautama, who is the Buddha.  Gautama founded the Buddhist tradition.  Buddha lived between roughly 563 and 483 BC.  Lumbini is one of four magnets for pilgrimage that sprang up in places pivotal to the life of the Buddha, the others being at Kushinagar, Bodh Gaya and Sarnath.

Aniruddha Mahathera | Aniruddha Mahathera (Devanagari: अनिरुद्ध महाथेरा) (born Gaja Ratna Tuladhar) (15 December 1915 – 17 February 2003) was a Nepalese Buddhist monk and the Sangha Nayak (Patriarch) of Nepal from 1998 until his death in 2003.  He was one of the most important figures in the revival of Theravada Buddhism in Nepal and the development of Lumbini, the Buddha's birthplace in southern Nepal, into a center of international pilgrimage.

Taulihawa, Nepal | Taulihawa, also known as Kapilavastu, is a municipality and administrative center of Kapilvastu District in Province No. 5 of southern Nepal.  Taulihawa is located roughly 25 km to the south-west of Lumbini, a UNESCO World Heritage Site and the birthplace of Gautama Buddha.

Lumbini Buddhist University | Lumbini Buddhist University is a tertiary educational institution currently under construction in Lumbini, Nepal, the birthplace of the Buddha.  The idea for the university was conceived at the First World Buddhist Summit held in Lumbini in 1998, and it was officially formed in November 2004.  The Lumbini Buddhist University Act promulgated on November 10, 2006 confirmed its legal status as well as setting out that the university would receive financial assistance from the government of Nepal.
response.summary_2: small town located in the Kapilvastu District, Lumbini, Nepal. Lumbini is a Buddhist pilgrimage site in the Rupandehi District of Nepal, traditionally considered the birthplace of Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha).
